evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully autonomous: do NOT greet, do NOT show a discovery menu, do NOT HALT for a [C] continue selection or any user approval. There is no existing project-context.md and no config to load — proceed straight through and produce the real project-context.md content.

Use the gds-generate-project-context skill to generate the project-context.md for this small Godot (GDScript) game. The relevant source is provided:
- evals/files/gds-gen-context/autoload/EventBus.gd
- evals/files/gds-gen-context/player/Player.gd
- evals/files/gds-gen-context/pickups/Coin.gd

Produce the project-context.md body: the critical, UNOBVIOUS rules and conventions an AI agent must follow when writing game code here. Focus on project-specific rules an agent would otherwise miss — not generic game-dev best practices.
evals.expected_output: A lean project-context.md surfacing the game's actual unobvious rules: (1) cross-system communication ALWAYS goes through the autoloaded EventBus singleton — nodes never call each other directly or get_node() into other systems; emit/subscribe via the bus signals (player_died, score_changed, coin_collected); (2) all movement/physics runs in _physics_process, never _process; (3) tunables (speed, jump_velocity, gravity, coin value) are @export vars, never literals baked into logic. It does NOT pad the file with generic advice (use object pooling, write tests, name things well) that any game would share.
expectations: It captures the signal-bus rule as a binding convention: cross-system communication must go through the autoloaded EventBus singleton (emit/subscribe to its signals), and nodes must not call across systems directly or reach into other systems with get_node().
It captures the rule that movement and physics must run in _physics_process, not _process.
It captures the convention that tunable values (speed, jump_velocity, gravity, coin value) are exposed as @export variables rather than magic numbers baked into the logic.

The captured rules are specific and unobvious — derived from THIS code (EventBus indirection, _physics_process, @export tunables) — rather than generic game-dev advice such as 'use object pooling', 'write tests', or 'keep frame time low'.
The output is lean and rule-focused (the project-context.md form) and does NOT pad itself with boilerplate best-practices that any game project would share.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully autonomous: do NOT greet, do NOT show a discovery menu, do NOT HALT for a [C] continue selection or any approval. Proceed straight through and produce the real project-context.md content.

Use the gds-generate-project-context skill on the same Godot game, but frame the rules as guidance an AI agent will follow when ADDING a new pickup type and a new moving entity. Source:
- evals/files/gds-gen-context/autoload/EventBus.gd
- evals/files/gds-gen-context/player/Player.gd
- evals/files/gds-gen-context/pickups/Coin.gd

Produce project-context.md rules that, if followed, would keep new code consistent with the existing patterns. Each rule should be concrete enough to act on, and tied to what the code actually does.
evals.expected_output: Actionable, code-grounded rules for extending the game consistently: a new pickup announces its effect by emitting a signal on EventBus (mirroring Coin's coin_collected) rather than calling the player/UI/manager directly, and new signals are declared on the EventBus; a new moving entity does its movement in _physics_process and uses move_and_slide on a CharacterBody2D; all of its tunables are @export vars, not literals. The rules are imperative and specific, not vague principles, and trace to the provided scripts.
expectations: The rules are phrased as concrete, actionable directives for adding content (e.g. 'a new pickup emits its effect on EventBus instead of calling the player/UI/manager directly; declare any new signal on the EventBus') rather than abstract principles.
It instructs that a new moving entity perform its movement in _physics_process (using move_and_slide on a CharacterBody2D), mirroring Player.gd, not in _process.
It instructs that new tunables be declared as @export variables rather than hard-coded literals, consistent with speed/jump_velocity/gravity/value in the existing scripts.
Each rule is traceable to something actually present in the three files (the EventBus signals, _physics_process movement, @export tunables) rather than invented conventions the code does not exhibit.
The output stays lean and project-specific and does not dilute the rules with generic best-practice filler that would apply to any Godot game.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully autonomous: do NOT greet, do NOT show a discovery menu, do NOT HALT for a [C] continue selection or any approval. Proceed straight through and produce the real project-context.md content.

Use the gds-generate-project-context skill to generate project-context.md for this small, plain GDScript file. Source:
- evals/files/gds-gen-context-plain/MathUtils.gd

This is ordinary, idiomatic GDScript with no unusual layering, no autoloads, no signal bus, and no project-specific conventions — just simple static helper functions. Produce project-context.md honestly: capture only genuinely unobvious, project-specific rules that an AI agent could miss. Do not manufacture rules.
evals.expected_output: An honest, minimal project-context.md: because the code embodies no unusual conventions, it records essentially nothing project-specific (at most noting it is plain idiomatic GDScript exposing static helpers on a class_name) and explicitly avoids padding the file with generic game-dev best-practices dressed up as project rules — e.g. it must NOT assert invented rules like 'always use object pooling', 'communicate via a signal bus', 'do physics in _physics_process', or fabricate an autoload/event architecture that this code does not have.
expectations: It recognizes that the code is plain, idiomatic GDScript with no unusual project-specific conventions, and keeps the project-context.md minimal/sparse accordingly — possibly stating outright that there are few or no unobvious rules to capture.

It does NOT pad the file with generic best-practice boilerplate ('use object pooling', 'communicate via signals', 'write tests', 'keep frame time low') presented as if they were this project's specific rules — manufacturing rules to fill the document is the failure mode under test.

It does NOT invent project-specific conventions absent from the code: no fictional EventBus/signal-bus mandate, no _physics_process movement rule, no @export-tunables convention, since MathUtils.gd contains only pure static helper functions.
Anything it does note is genuinely grounded in the file (e.g. static functions on a class_name extending RefCounted), not asserted without basis.
The result honors the skill's intent — a lean file of unobvious, must-follow rules — by staying honest and short rather than producing volume for its own sake.
